does: This paper applies sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to extract and interpret the internal representations of three different EEG foundation models—SleepFM, REVE, and LaBraM—that perform well on clinical tasks but remain largely opaque. The authors decompose model embeddings into sparse feature dictionaries, benchmark these features against a clinical taxonomy covering abnormality, age, sex, and medication to assess monosemanticity and entanglement across architectures, and develop a unified hyperparameter tuning procedure validated by an intrinsic dictionary health audit. Through concept steering experiments, they introduce a probe area metric to measure steering selectivity and classify features into three operational regimes: selectively steerable, encoded but entangled, and non-encoded. The work uncovers critical representational failures including global performance collapse under certain interventions and problematic clinical entanglements such as age-pathology confounding that cannot be disentangled.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability program established by recent SAE-based audits of large language models to the domain of EEG foundation models and clinical AI. It builds on prior sparse autoencoder research that decomposes neural representations into interpretable features, adapting these techniques to medical signal processing where clinical grounding is essential. The paper responds to growing concerns about the opacity of foundation models in healthcare, echoing broader efforts to understand and validate deep learning in clinical contexts. It combines concept steering methods from neuroscience-inspired interpretability with clinical domain knowledge, leveraging established frameworks for evaluating representation quality like monosemanticity assessment. The cross-architecture comparison reflects recent recognition that interpretability findings should be robust across different model designs rather than architecture-specific artifacts.
enables: This framework opens pathways for clinically trustworthy deployment of EEG foundation models by exposing which learned representations can be reliably controlled and which contain problematic entanglements that resist intervention. Future work can build on the identified operational regimes to design architectures or training procedures that minimize wrecking-ball effects and clinical confounding. The concept steering methodology and probe area metric establish quantitative tools for evaluating disentanglement in medical AI systems, potentially extending to other clinical modalities. Understanding age-pathology and other confoundings documented here motivates both interventions in model training and post-hoc correction strategies. The transferable hyperparameter procedure suggests that mechanistic interpretability tooling can be standardized across diverse medical foundation models, supporting broader adoption of interpretable-by-design approaches in clinical AI and enabling clinicians to identify when model predictions rest on spurious or dangerously entangled representations.
